text: Increased hip adduction during running is a risk factor for running-related injury in female recreational runners.
Reduced peak rearfoot eversion during running is a risk factor for running-related injury in female recreational runners.
Biomechanical risk factors for running-related injury differ depending on the runner's sex and competition level, meaning risk factors identified in one population may not apply to another.

Biomechanical risk factors identified in retrospective injury studies may not reflect true causal relationships; prospective designs with at least 12 weeks of follow-up are needed to establish causation.

Beginning runners and military recruits show different biomechanical injury risk profiles than established recreational or competitive runners, and findings from one group should not be generalized to the other.
